data.title: PayPal: Keeping It As A Possible Convexity Play For Two Reasons (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
PayPal Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:PYPL) is a widely debated stock due to factors such as its inconsistent performance in recent years and a pending shift in its corporate strategy. I previously covered PayPal in March, 2025, where I communicated a cautious outlook to readers on the basis of discretionary spending headwinds and the stock's questionable pricing metrics. Despite my previously cautious outlook, I've decided to turn to a bullish outlook on PayPal's stock; here's why.

Reason 1: Oversold and Possibly Underestimated

PayPal has shed over 80% of its market value in the past 5-years. Its underperformance started in late 2021 amid a lack of conviction in its growth trajectory. In my view, investors grew discontent with the company's lack of new account growth. In addition, a bear market unfolded in 2022; it seems as though PayPal never recovered afterwards due to a lack of optimism among investors.

Figure 2 illustrates PayPal's historical stock performance. The stock rallied intensely during the COVID-era tech boom. However, I think investors eventually priced froth out of the market and ultimately decided to avoid PayPal's stock after the 2022 bear market ended. I believe investors neglected the stock post bear market due to PayPal's relatively lower new account monetisation. Moreover, a credit scare occurred within the banking industry in 2022/23, which likely contributed to PayPal's demise, seeing as it rolled out a Buy-Now-Pay-Later program in the years leading up to the credit stress environment.

Roll forward to 2026 and we see PayPal's stock trading at $44.21 per share, slightly above its listing price in 2015. I think most of PayPal's recent bad news has been priced-in and it's time to start looking at a possible reversal. However, the question beckons: Does PayPal have enough fundamental strength to recover from its lows and/or will a catalyst emerge? I think so; here's why.

PayPal has decided to enter a new regime by hiring Enrique Lores. However, let's focus on incumbent performance before discussing Lores. As illustrated by figure 3, PayPal's nominal growth isn't declining. The company's FX-neutral total payment volume increased by 6% during fiscal 2025 amid rising consistency in consumer demand as well as its omni-channel set-up and low-fee solutions (Venmo). The company has successfully translated its higher volumes into revenue growth, delivering 4% year-over-year growth during fiscal 2025, accommodated by transaction margin growth of 6% and non-GAAP EPS of $5.31 (+14% year-over-year). In my view, PayPal's omni-channel and synergies from integrated platforms have led to consolidated growth.

Let's take a closer look at PayPal's fundamentals.

The company currently derives around 43% of its revenue from international transactions, which I find highly appealing, given the emergence of capital fragmentation in the geo-political landscape (PayPal might provide a workaround) and the growing representation of off-shored contract work. I see tremendous opportunity in cross-border flow and international payments. However, I'm slightly concerned by PayPal's take-rate of 1.82%, which has been flat since it broke below 2% in March 2021. Take rates refer to the amount of transactions that PayPal retains as revenue from fees. A declining take rate shows that power might've shifted to merchants amid rising competition in the digital payments arena, which PayPal owns ~45% of (data may differ between vendors). Despite a declining trend in take rates, we've seen PayPal enhance footprint, volume, and total revenue growth on a consistent basis in recent years. In addition, I think the company's Buy Now Pay Later offering will eventually find its place - consumer credit can be highly cyclical and market consolidation is challenging – if mastered, we could see BNPL contribute massively.

Collectively, PayPal's fundamentals have translated into appetising profitability metrics, which can be used for both fundamental analysis as well as stock screening. For example, on a 5-year normalised basis, we see PayPal's return on common equity at 20.13% without any signs of deterioration. Furthermore, the company's 5-year average EBITDA margin of 19.45% is attractive and in-line with the sector median.

We've seen price multiple compression occur since PayPal's stock started selling off in late 2021. Similar to profitability ratios, multiples can be used for both fundamental analysis and stock screening. Many avoid using P/E multiples for tech companies due to the subjective nature of amortisation accounting and tech firms' inconsistent capital structures. However, I think PayPal can be priced on a P/E multiple due to its mature capital structure and its consistent depreciation & amortization schedule (which has ranged between 2.9% - 3.6% of net revenue in the past three fiscal years). Using that logic, we can see that PayPal's forward-GAAP P/E ratio of 8.78x is substantially lower than its 5-year average of 31.22x. In addition, PayPal's price-to-cash flow, price-to-sales, and EV/EBITDA ratios are all materially lower than their 5-year averages. Lastly, I point to the fact that PayPal's balance sheet illustrates a current ratio of 1.29x and $8.05 billion in cash & equivalents (which covers ~13.44% of its total liabilities), suggesting low liquidity risk, meaning the company can weather a storm if needed, re-invest internally for growth, or acquire externally for market share. In addition, solvency risk arguably remains intact, echoed by PayPal's total assets/total liabilities ratio of ~1.34 (a distance-to-default proxy); sustainable EBITDA and a commendable distance-to-default can manage both credit and equity investors' perception of the business' real risk.

If PayPal's Fundamentals Are Commendable. Then why is it selling off?

I would say that PayPal's recent underperformance can be attributed to a combination of recency bias from investors and complacency from the company itself. Regarding the latter, PayPal seems content with sitting on its market share instead of expanding aggresively, conveyed by the fact that its CapEx is relatively low versus its shareholder distribution amount (PayPal has guided toward $1 billion in CapEx for 2026 and $6 billion in shareholder returns). While shareholder returns can be accretive, it might ultimately be dilutive when a company distributes as opposed to re-investing during a period of intense competition – In my view, competition in the digital payments environment is rising; I think investors believe PayPal needs to re-invest to sustain its market share. Furthermore, I partially attribute PayPal's lacklustre stock performance to a potential recency bias from investors. PayPal's recent growth is steady, yet lower than the growth market participants had become accustomed to in the company's earlier days. For example, I point to figure 8, which shows that PayPal's 5-year revenue and EBITDA growth has levelled off considerably versus the ten year compound annual growth horizon. While I understand the discontent with slower growth, I think investors have over-emphasised the fact; an 80%+ 5-year sell-off is arguably exaggerated.

Reason 2: A Possible Acquisition Target

Rumours have emerged that PayPal might be an acquisition target. For example, recent reports linked Stripe with a takeover bid, driven by the fact that PayPal's market capitalisation has dropped to ~40.7 billion, which is arguably quite low for a company generating over $33 billion in sales, especially if we consider the company's telling market share, abundance of intellectual property (workforce and industry knowledge), and synergy prospects.

Side note: Figure 9 breaks down PayPal's enterprise value.

Why do I consider PayPal an acquisition target?

I don't think Stripe is the only company who would be interested in acquiring PayPal. In fact, I'd argue that mega cap companies such as Amazon (AMZN) who carry growing representation might be interested due to market share gain and synergies. Furthermore, speculation has emerged that Enrique Lores, PayPal's new CEO, might deploy a streamlining strategy during his tenure at PayPal. I think an execution-first strategy might add marginal value to the company, providing acquirers with a solid roll-up opportunity, whereby they can incorporate the infrastructure as-in, as opposed to worrying about loose-ends. In addition, streamlining can make PayPal a more alluring takeover target. Why? Well, a growth-by-acquisition strategy might've inflated the balance sheet. Instead, a stream-lined strategy turns PayPal into a more nimble organisation, allowing for a cleaner set-up in takeover bids down-the-line.

My acquisition target argument isn't central to my bullish thesis. However, I deem it a factor worth considering.

Unmentioned Risk Factors

I mentioned a few risks throughout the article. However, I wanted to highlight a few additional risks in a discreet section.

PayPal's Streamlining Might Backfire

As previously mentioned, PayPal's streamlining can result in sustainability of profitability metrics. In addition, streamlining can trim the balance sheet, leaving it as a more alluring acquisition target. That being said, streamlining always runs the risk of market share decay, especially during an era of rising competition in the digital payments arena.

What Goes Down Doesn't Always Come Back Up

Though often heterogeneously interpreted, hysteresis refers to a permanent effect on a variable resulting from an event. In my other words, it refers to a permanent change in equilibrium. As such, I highlight the fact that PayPal's stock won't revert to mean by default; the market might've simply priced a new equilibrium.

Final Word

I'm hanging on to my PayPal stock. Despite the myriad of headwinds faced by the company in recent years, a case can be made that investors have exaggerated the near-term slowdown in growth while ignoring features such as PayPal's market share, profitability, and normalised valuation multiples. In addition, a subtle case can be made for PayPal as an acquisition target, adding a secondary layer to my bullish outlook.

I label PayPal a convexity play because I believe its downside risk is mostly priced; a catalyst might lead to telling upside.

data.title: U.S. Inflation Is Rising, The Fed Is Waiting, And Forecasts Are Getting Weaker
data.description:
By Krzysztof Kamiński

Economists have clearly downgraded their outlook for the U.S. economy, concluding that the war with Iran and rising energy prices will weigh increasingly heavily on economic activity in the coming months. At the center of these concerns are higher inflation, weaker consumption, slower GDP growth, and a deteriorating labor market.

More and more often, the conflict in the Middle East is being viewed not as a temporary disruption, but as a new inflationary shock that could make it harder to maintain a balance between growth and price stability.

Inflation is once again becoming a bigger problem

According to a Bloomberg survey, the average forecast for growth in the PCE index - one of the most important measures of inflation in the U.S. - has risen for this year to 3.1% from the previous 2.6%.

This is a significant shift, showing that economists are beginning to assume a more lasting impact of higher energy prices on prices across the entire economy. An additional warning signal comes from the OECD, which expects average inflation in G20 countries to reach 4% this year, whereas as recently as December it had projected 2.8%.

At present, the conflict is having its strongest impact through the fuel and energy markets. Gasoline prices in the U.S. have risen by more than 30% this month, to around $4 per gallon, marking the biggest jump since Hurricane Katrina in 2005.

Higher fuel and transportation costs are already increasing the burden on households, and in the coming months they may also translate into higher food prices and prices for other consumer goods. There are also concerns about fertilizer shortages, which could push prices in stores even higher.

Economic growth is weakening, and the labor market is sending worse signals

The worsening outlook is also visible in economic growth forecasts. Economists now expect U.S. GDP to grow by 2.3% this year, compared with the earlier forecast of 2.5%. The scale of the revision does not yet suggest a sharp downturn, but it does confirm that the economy is beginning to feel the effects of rising energy costs and greater uncertainty more clearly.

Weaker forecasts also apply to the labor market. The expected average monthly number of new jobs has been revised down to 43,000 from 70,000, while the average unemployment rate is projected to reach 4.5%. This means that the labor market should remain relatively stable, but the pace of improvement is expected to be much weaker than previously assumed.

At the same time, the probability of a recession over the next 12 months has increased from 25% to 30%, showing that concerns about the economy’s condition are becoming more widespread.

The Fed may wait longer before cutting interest rates

Higher inflation and greater uncertainty have also affected expectations for the Federal Reserve. Economists have pushed back their forecast for the first interest rate cut to September, concluding that the Fed will need more time to assess whether the rise in energy prices is temporary or whether it is beginning to spread more permanently throughout the economy.

This is an important change, because until recently the market had been counting on faster monetary easing that could support consumption and investment.

Wall Street is lowering expectations, but it is not yet talking about a crisis

A more cautious view is also evident among the largest financial institutions. Goldman Sachs assesses the risk of recession at 30% and assumes that the unemployment rate will rise to 4.6% by the end of 2026.

Morgan Stanley, meanwhile, has lowered its forecast for consumption growth in 2026 to 1.7% from 2.0%, indicating that higher energy prices will largely offset the positive impact of larger tax refunds.

Even so, a deep downturn scenario is not yet dominant. Some economists believe that the U.S. economy may still grow at around 2% in 2026, mainly thanks to investments in data centers and the artificial intelligence sector.

These are areas less dependent on expensive imported energy than traditional industries. However, this scenario is resting on an increasingly narrow foundation, as it depends mainly on sustained investor optimism toward AI and on the resilience of spending by wealthier consumers.

Consumption is still holding up, but that resilience may be limited

So far, consumer behavior data do not show a clear breakdown. JPMorgan and Bank of America indicate that by mid-March there were no strong signs of a slowdown in credit card spending. This suggests that American households are still maintaining relatively solid purchasing activity.

Economists note, however, that even a quick end to the conflict would not necessarily mean a quick disappearance of its effects. The impact on the oil market, transportation, and production costs may persist much longer, sustaining inflationary pressure and limiting the strength of any rebound.

The U.S. economy is becoming more fragile

The current picture of the American economy does not yet point to a sharp deterioration in conditions, but it clearly shows the system’s growing fragility. Higher energy prices are pushing inflation upward, weaker labor market and GDP forecasts are weighing on sentiment, and the Fed may be forced to maintain restrictive monetary policy for longer.

As a result, the U.S. economy remains on a growth path, but it is increasingly dependent on a few narrow pillars, such as AI investment and the relative resilience of consumption. The war with Iran is therefore beginning to act as a new destabilizing force - one that is not yet triggering a recession, but is clearly increasing the risk that the economy will become less resilient to future shocks.

Original Post

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Plunges 6% Amid Energy Price Spikes And Market Volatility
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Driven by a volatile mix of escalating gas prices and financial market instability, consumer sentiment plunged nearly 6% in March to its lowest level since late 2025.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index fell 3.7 points to 53.3, missing the 55.5 forecast and snapping a four-month streak of gains as consumers grapple with the fallout from the ongoing conflict in Iran. This sharp decline places current sentiment in the bottom 1st percentile of the series' history.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regard to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month: a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment fell back 6% this month to its lowest level since December 2025. Declines were seen across age and political party. Consumers with middle and higher incomes and stock wealth, buffeted by both escalating gas prices and volatile financial markets in the wake of the Iran conflict, exhibited particularly large drops in sentiment. Overall, the short-run economic outlook plunged 14%, and year-ahead expected personal finances sank 10%, while declines in long-run expectations were more subdued. These patterns suggest that, at this time, consumers may not expect recent negative developments to persist far into the future. These views are subject to change, however, if the Iran conflict becomes protracted or if higher energy prices pass through to overall inflation. Interviews for this release were collected between February 17 and March 23, with about two-thirds completed after the start of the US military conflict in Iran.

A Historical Perspective on Recession Risk

The chart below provides a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We have highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession. The current level of 53.3 is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 36.5% below its average reading of 84.0 (arithmetic mean) and 35.6% below its geometric mean of 82.8, based on data dating back to 1978.

To visualize the volatility and its impact on the broader economy, the following chart includes a three-month moving average and real GDP. Historically, prolonged periods where the moving average remains at these depressed levels have closely correlated with negative GDP growth (the red bars below).

The Political and Presidential Lens

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. Sentiment is often viewed through a partisan lens, but the data shows that sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

As the chart below illustrates, the current "plunge" is a rare moment of bipartisan agreement, with declines seen across the political spectrum as energy costs hit every household.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

While both measures fell in March, a concerning gap is opening between how consumers feel today and what they fear for tomorrow.

  • Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) fell for the first time in three months to 55.8, a 1.4% decline from the previous month and a 12.5% drop from a year ago. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 57.8.
  • Consumer Expectations (CEI) fell a second straight month to 51.7, an 8.7% decline from the previous month and a 1.7% drop from one year ago. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 54.1.

The steeper decline in the Expectations Index suggests that while consumers are managing current costs, they are bracing for a much harsher economic environment in the months ahead.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations climbed from 3.4% in February to 3.8% this month, the largest one-month increase since April 2025. The current reading exceeds those seen in 2024 and remains well above the 2.3-3.0% range seen in the two years pre-pandemic. Long-run inflation expectations inched down to 3.2%. In 2024, values ranged between 2.8% and 3.2%, while in 2019 and 2020, they were consistently below 2.8%. Note that for both time horizons, interviews completed after February 28th exhibited higher inflation expectations than those completed before that date.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB Business Optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: How Too Many Drivers Spoil UGL
data.description:
Investment Thesis

ProShares Ultra Gold ETF (UGL) is a classic example of how too many good cooks can spoil the broth. Gold’s monster movement in the last 12-18 months was caused by not one or two but four entangled factors. These four drivers are rates falling or expected rate cuts, massive central bank price-agnostic buying, numerous geopolitical risks, wars, etc., and dollar debasement. Even a single one of these factors is enough to move gold up. When you combine them, you get a motley group of forces - some of them tangential if not downright opposed to each other. That dynamic is enough to cause loss of momentum sometimes.

For gold itself, that occasional loss of momentum is not a problem because gold moves primarily on direction, not path. For a 2x daily leveraged ETF like UGL, though, even a little loss of gold’s momentum in a bullish market creates a situation where gold moves in a zigzag manner - even if broadly upwards. That sort of choppiness works fine for physical gold but is fatal for daily leveraged ETFs because they follow the path, not the direction of movement. This is why, after a terrific year where it saw a ~77% rise, UGL is down ~30% this month.

My thesis is that, due to both structural reasons and the effect of macro factors on that structure, UGL will not bounce back easily. The structural constraints of UGL are well-known. The macro reason, which I will discuss here, is the presence of too many drivers at once. Unless there’s a clean source of momentum, momentum will not be steady, and since I believe that is not going to happen soon, I rate UGL a sell.

Why These Drivers No Longer Work Together

Take rates: rates are not falling in a straight line anymore. Inflation is not moving in any direction evenly, and policy—or at least its expectation—keeps shifting. One authority talks about rate cuts, another equally strong authority discusses pauses.

The same uncertainty exists with the dollar. It is no longer cleanly weakening. At times, it strengthens when there are risk-off flows and relative growth. At other times, it weakens on policy expectations. So what we get is a push-pull dynamic, not a consistent tailwind.

So is the geopolitical situation. It is not all constantly bad. Yes, risk is definitely elevated, but they are episodic, not cumulative. Sometimes one event escalates, and markets react sharply, like in the ongoing Iran conflict today. Another major event fades from public perception, or from headlines—the Russia-Ukraine war, Venezuela, and President Maduro—and that softens the momentum for gold. So what we get are short bursts of momentum, not sustained trends.

Perhaps the most stable of these four drivers is central bank buying. However, it is also the least sensitive to price. That means it provides a floor, one below which the gold price will not fall. But it fails to provide a directional push.

Put together, these drivers no longer reinforce each other. They take turns dominating the narrative, often in opposite directions. The result is not a breakdown in the gold thesis, but a breakdown in its consistency. Gold is no longer moving in a clean trend but in a sequence of reactions.

That distinction is critical. A single dominant driver creates persistence. Multiple competing drivers create volatility.

Why That Is Specifically A Problem For UGL

UGL is designed for persistence, not for alternating momentum.

When gold is driven by a single dominant factor, its upward movement is sustained and consistent. Here, UGL’s 2x leveraged daily reset structure does its job as intended, amplifying the returns. However, when so many competing forces line up and gold’s upward movement becomes erratic, UGL’s structure amplifies not that direction, but that erratic path.

This is how UGL starts underperforming. Since its returns are tied to daily movements, not overall trends, alternating gains and losses in gold only create losses in UGL. Over time, this gets compounded.

UGL Is Built For Direction - Not Volatility

UGL’s problem is not gold’s broad direction, which is still moving upward, but its own daily-return structure. The fund targets twice the daily return of the Bloomberg Gold Subindex. That means gold’s erratic upward path has a telling effect on UGL’s returns.

UGL is not facing some minor tracking issue—the issue is structural and consistent with the very nature of leveraged daily return ETFs. Such returns are path dependent, so even if the broad direction of gold is positive, daily movement patterns—gold going up and down intraday - will hurt UGL’s returns.

This is straightforward: suppose gold moves +5% one day and -5% the next day; it ends slightly down, say ~-0.25%. This is basic math. A 2x leveraged ETF just compounds this loss, so it becomes -0.5% or thereabouts. Keep on doing this every few days, and the gap between UGL and physical gold or plain gold ETFs keeps widening.

So when we have two things, higher volatility and longer holding periods, returns tend to be worse than the benchmark. That is exactly the setup we are now in.

On top of that structural drag, how UGL tracks gold is also responsible for its performance. It does not hold physical gold but uses futures contracts as well as swap agreements. Now, futures contracts expire and have to be repurchased, often at higher prices. This is called roll cost.

Roll cost shows up as a measurable drag over time. In a typical contango environment, each roll can cause a small loss because each future contract becomes more expensive than the earlier one.

Moving on, swap agreements are done with third-party institutions; they are not directly held. This introduces counterparty costs and reliability concerns. Tracking friction is the difference between holding gold and holding a derivative like a futures contract; this gets compounded over time. Add the 0.95% expense ratio, and what you have is not a direct macro view of gold, but something more besides that. That extra cost degrades returns significantly over time, unless you are buying at the right time and under the best volatility conditions. Additionally, holding this ETF for more than a day has its own risks that include volatility drag, on which more can be found here.

Why Bounce-Backs Will Be Short-Lived

A basic counter would be: if gold is still broadly bullish, why wouldn’t UGL simply recover along with it?

To that, I would say gold rebounds are likely going to be fragmented, not sustained. Each driver of its momentum—rates, geopolitics, central bank buying, and currency weakness—operates on its own timeline. One may turn supportive, but since they are all entangled with each other in complex ways, they will not all become simultaneously supportive.

So now we get a start-stop pattern. We see gold rally sharply on rate cut talk, then stall as yields stabilize. It can spike when bombs are falling, and it can retrace when another ship crosses the Strait of Hormuz safely, leaving gold modestly upward moving but dragging UGL down due to its daily structure.

The current environment is very different, with its volatility and sentiment swings. Leveraged products perform poorly in such times.

Why A Single Dominant Driver Is Unlikely

UGL needs a cleaner, less diversified macro trigger. This cycle saw too many powerful triggers, some bearish, some bullish. The result was choppy movement. Gold may still be fine, but choppiness is fatal for UGL.

These are not just multiple drivers of gold. They are conflicting and competing drivers.

Take rates and inflation as an example. Gold goes typically bullish when real yields decline. But real yields will now decline when central banks ease policy, and they will do that if inflation stabilizes. If inflation does not stabilize, rate cuts are not going to happen now. On the other hand, if growth weakens, there will be rate cuts, but there will be parallel risk-off moves that will make the dollar stronger. So rate cuts will drive gold higher, but a strong dollar will drag it down. So even within this one driver, the path is not linear.

Now let’s look at the dollar, which often trades in the opposite direction to gold. However, the dollar itself is being driven by too many things - rate expectations, differences in growth, and geopolitical risk sentiment. This is creating a situation where gold and the dollar are actually moving together. That adds to the inconsistency.

The key problem is not a multiplicity of drivers, but that they are fundamentally different in time and by their functions. Monetary policy is slow-moving and inflation-driven. Geopolitical events are increasingly whimsical, moving in sudden bursts, then fading. Currency markets react to both broad geopolitics and policy expectations. So, they react to two different things that have no correlation to each other. Central banks buy hundreds of tons of gold, no matter the price. So they offer support but do not push.

Because these are so different, I don’t believe their natural tendency is to align. Even when they do align, it is incidental and does not persist. That structural mismatch makes sustained alignment unlikely, not just absent at the moment.

What Would Change My View

Three things need to happen together for UGL to work properly—path, positioning, and cleaner triggers. One, gold must move up, and do so in an orderly fashion. Leveraged products with daily-reset characteristics need not just the right direction, but the right path. When gold rises up in a straight line, UGL can do its job well, giving you nearly 2x daily return.

The single dominant driver that could do this and control the narrative could be real yields. This is my most plausible candidate. A sustained and decisive decline in real yields could take hold of the gold movement and make it trending. Not intermittent rate cuts, but a persistent, planned easing cycle combined with stable inflation. This could create a consistent tailwind for gold.

The other requirement is positioning. Gold got its recent support from a powerful narrative—falling rates, central bank buying, geopolitics, and dollar debasement. However, the narrative became widely understood, so there was the crowd, and then a little loss of momentum got amplified.

This needs to change. Gold must stop being so interesting again for UGL to work properly. That reset has to come from either a larger correction, or we just have to give it time. Until that reset happens, though, UGL remains vulnerable to crowded, profit-taking, flow-driven swings.

Conclusion

There is no doubt that UGL’s returns have been strong. But the setup has now shifted. Gold is now over-owned, a widely held macro trade, and we can see that in the price action. UGL is not built for this kind of environment.

It doesn’t matter if gold drifts higher - many investors will say that, but that is not the true UGL investment argument. What matters is whether gold is taking a zigzag path upwards. Since that is happening, UGL is not the right gold instrument.

UGL will probably become attractive again when gold trends consistently upward. This is not that time. So this is not UGL’s time.

Until there is a single dominant driver pushing gold in a sustained way, and until positioning resets enough to allow that trend to build cleanly, UGL remains structurally disadvantaged.

Since I don’t believe this set of four drivers will align in the near future, I consider UGL a clear SELL.

data.title: Credit Markets Are Still Risk-On: Why We're Calling A Strong Sell
data.description:
Thesis

This Seeking Alpha article is our tool to warn investors of the risks we see in the corporate bond market, especially in situations where we see a high probability for those risks to materialise. We argue that investors should consider high expected inflation numbers, current low spreads compared to the last +20 years, and the low credit quality of investment-grade bond ETFs when allocating their portfolio. We initiate with a STRONG SELL on corporate bonds as an asset class by analyzing in detail multiple popular ETFs with that exposure.

Credit Spreads

In our previous article, The Silent Risk In Portfolios: Long-Duration Exposure, we argued that the unfavorable maturity distribution of US Treasury debt, high US budget deficits, and inflation expectations derived from breakeven rates are in favor of selling long-duration Treasury and buying ultra-short duration Treasury. Since the bond market is generally priced with a spread on risk-free, i.e., Treasury bonds in the case of US corporate bonds, in this article, we will look at the corporate bond ETFs and then give our final verdict on the total bond market ETFs.

The chart below shows corporate bond spreads over time vs. Treasury 10-year bonds on a yield basis. As the data clearly shows, all stated spreads per rating category are far below its median levels for the period. Spread on US corporate A-rated securities is 1.02% vs the median of 0.78% or 24 bps below the historic median. Spread on BBB/Baa corporates is at 1.12% vs the median of 1.61% or 49 bps below the median, and spread on HY is 2.99% vs 3.85% median or 86 bps below the median.

So, the lower the credit quality, the greater the deviation from the median. In our view, this shows that the corporate bond market is still in a risk-on environment, no matter the poor returns over the last couple of years that have happened due to the bear market in Treasuries. Also, please note that in the case of market stress, spreads can move quickly over the median line, and during the recession (marked on the chart in red), they can reach more than 1.000 bps.

In our view, this data shows that investors are not adequately compensated for the risk in the corporate bond market, although the current yield, which may be much higher than in previous years, may feel much better.

So, let us check the most popular corporate bond ETF by investment-grade categories.

Investment Grade Corporate Bonds ETFs

Investment-grade corporate bonds are bonds issued by companies with credit ratings of BBB- or higher by S&P Global Ratings by S&P Global Inc. (SPGI) and by privately held Fitch Ratings, or Baa3 or higher by Moody's provided by Moody's Corporation (MCO), indicating relatively low default risk. They typically offer lower yields than high-yield bonds, reflecting stronger balance sheets with more stable cash flows and a higher probability of timely debt repayment.

The most popular ETFs in this category are Vanguard Short-Term Corporate Bond Idx Fd ETF (VCSH), iShares iBoxx $ Inv Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) and iShares Broad USD Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (USIG) and the smaller one PIMCO Investment Grade Corporate Bond Index ETF (CORP).

iShares iBoxx $ Inv Grade Corporate Bond ETF

This is ETF with total net assets of $30 billion, with more details in the following table.

Please be aware that the allocation of the fund is at the lower end of the investment grade credit rating, with a BBB - rated corporates making more than 40% of the total allocation. By our calculation stated before, BBB - rated corporate bonds would need to rise in yields for about 49 bps just to get in line with the median. In a case of severe market stress, spreads would widen even further, and in the case of recession, we would expect a large number of downgrades and defaults that would be a serious drag on the investors.

Due to our view of bear-steepening US Treasuries yield curve, historically low spreads, and low quality of the investment grade bonds, we rate iShares iBoxx $ Inv Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) as STRONG SELL.

iShares Broad USD Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF

This is the fund with about $17 billion of total net assets. Compared to the previous one, it offers much larger diversification with 11.4 k holdings vs 3.1 k on the previous one. It has a slightly shorter duration of 6.37 years compared to 7.92. In our view, the duration difference is not large enough to influence investor decisions in a meaningful way when comparing the two. The average YTM of 5.13% vs 5.27% on the latter is also not that significant.

What worries us the most is that this ETF is even more tilted to the verge of investment grade, with a BBB-rated bonds containing 45% of the total exposure. Top, AAA-rated bonds are less than 1% of the exposure.

Same as in the previous case, due to our view of bear-steepening US Treasuries yield curve, historically low spreads, and low quality of the investment-grade bonds in the in the assets, we initiate iShares Broad USD Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (USIG) with STRONG SELL.

PIMCO Investment Grade Corporate Bond Index ETF

We stated in the previous part of the article that this is a much smaller ETF compared to the previous two, with a net asset value of $1.6 billion. The duration of 6.5 years is not much different from the previous ones, same as the YTM of 5.27%.

However, here we find a more granular view of the portfolio's credit rating.

Although stated as a corporate bond ETF, the ETF holds 8% of government-related securities. We believe this is due to more flexibility for PM-s since it is a smaller ETF, and since most of the bonds are traded in minimal lots (100 thousand or more), and as a source of quick liquidity. Like its stated peers, however, it has a poor credit quality with 43.85 % of total assets in the BBB-rated range. What is most interesting is that 10.68% of the ETF assets are at the very edge of investment grade, at BBB-. Also, a disproportionately large part of the not-rated securities of more that 15% is a source of concern per se, in our view.

Due to the same reasons as previous ETFs, we initiate PIMCO Investment Grade Corporate Bond Index ETF (CORP) as STRONG SELL.

Vanguard Short-Term Corporate Bond Idx Fd ETF

This ETF has total net assets of $48.3 billion according to the latest data available. In bond strategies, short-term is often considered 1-3 years, so this fund, with an average maturity of 2.9 years and an average duration of 2.7, is on the upper limit of short-term. We believe this is to enhance yield, which currently states 4.2%.

Same as the previous examples, the fund is largely exposed to the BBB-rated segment of the bond market with 46.05% of total exposure. AAA-rated securities are 0.31% of the total fund, something we find to be too aggressive for this kind of ETF.

Due to the same reasons as previous ETFs, we initiate Vanguard Short-Term Corporate Bond Idx Fd ETF (VCSH) with STRONG SELL.

High-Yield Corporate Bond ETFs

High-yield corporate bonds are debt securities issued by companies with below investment-grade credit ratings, offering higher yields to compensate for elevated credit risk. The limit for high-yield corporate bonds is a credit rating below investment grade, which is defined as lower than BBB− by Standard & Poor's and Fitch Ratings, or lower than Baa3 by Moody's Corporation (MCO). They are typically issued by firms with higher leverage or less stable cash flows, which increases the probability of default.

The most popular ETF in this category is iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY).

iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF

This ETF has total net assets of $24.5 billion. The risk of the portfolio is mitigated partially by its lower duration than in the case of investment-grade funds. Here, the duration is only 3 years, while the average YTM is 7.32%. However, one needs to be careful while taking YTM into account because, in the case of any bond default, the real YTM will be lower.

Unlike the investment-grade ETFs, where fund assets are exposed to the lower part of the stated range, here we can see that the majority of exposure is to the upper-tier of HY space, with BB-rated bonds making more than half of the portfolio.

However, since we believe that HY issuers are at high risk of raising interest rates as well as widening spreads, which could make the cost of debt hard to service, we view iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY) as STRONG SELL. We simply do not think that in a situation where inflation is expected to rise substantially and where HY spreads are under the LT median, investors are not compensated enough for the risk they would take on with this product.

Broad Bond Market ETFs

Finally, broad bond market ETFs are the ones that combine security and liquidity from Treasuries that make up about 3/4 of total assets and higher yield from corporates that make up the rest of the portfolio. The most popular ETF in this category is the iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG).

iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF

This is ETF with total assets of $138 billion, duration of 5.76 years and average YTM of 4.6%. Since the bear market in US Treasuries has lasted for some time, the fund has a momentum grade of D.

Although we can see the selling point of these kinds of products, with the majority of assets in Treasuries with enhanced risk from corporates that still puts it in the bond and not equity bucket, we believe that the current situation we mentioned before (rising inflation, US unfavorable debt maturities, and corporate spreads under the median) exposes bond investors to equity-like risk they need to be aware of. As shown in the rating quality allocation, BBB-rated bonds account for 11.87% of the portfolio.

Due to everything mentioned above, we initiate iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) with STRONG SELL. Again, we urge investors to recognize equity-like risk from the BBB-rated part of the portfolio that can widen spreads and start to turn bad rather quickly in case of a recession.

Risk to our Bearish View

The main risk to our bearish view is a stark change of policy regarding budget spending and deficit. If policymakers decide to cut the deficit and cut spending in a prolonged and sustainable way, that would give the market more confidence that long-term inflation is manageable, and finally, it would give the Fed more room to cut rates. That would not only cut rates and lower the yields but possibly even cut spreads for corporate bonds.

The other risk to our view is the Fed controlling the curve and effectively cutting the rates while viewing the inflation rates as transitory. A major domestic economic shock would force the Fed to move preemptively and cut rates, no matter the current inflation levels. However, this is a risk to bear our bearish view, contingent on the move in Treasury yields and spreads not widening any further.

To wrap It Up

After we argued that the unfavorable maturity distribution of US Treasury debt, high US budget deficits, and inflation expectations derived from breakeven rates are in favor of selling long-duration Treasury and buying ultra-short-duration Treasury, we wanted to see what risks investors face when investing in corporate bonds. As this article clearly concludes, there are multiple layers of risk that investors may not be aware of. One of the risks is tight spreads across the different rating categories compared to the historical median. The lower the investment quality, the lower the spread compared to the median. The other important risk is that, as we clearly demonstrated, investment-grade corporate bond ETFs are largely exposed to the lowest part of the investment-grade universe that carries the highest yield but also the highest risk.

Also, we believe that HY corporate bond ETFs are disproportionately exposed to equity-like risks with widening spreads and rising inflation that can make large numbers of companies insolvent and make them default, so stated YTM on those ETFs may be much higher than yields investors will be able to realize with those ETFs.

Finally, due to everything stated in the article, we argue that investors in broad-based bond ETFs should realize the level of equity-like risks they are taking since the ETF carries a large part of the lowest investment-grade securities.

We initialize all stated ETFs, Vanguard Short-Term Corporate Bond Idx Fd ETF (VCSH), iShares iBoxx $ Inv Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) and iShares Broad USD Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (USIG) and the smaller one PIMCO Investment Grade Corporate Bond Index ETF (CORP), iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY) and iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) as STRONG SELL.

data.title: Weekly Market Compass: No. 13, Geopolitical Risk Sets The Pace
data.description:
The past week has seen stock markets experience strong swings due to geopolitical events in the Middle East. The key event of the week could be President Trump's announcement that he will suspend attacks on Iranian oil facilities for five days and presented a 15-point peace plan to help resolve the conflict. Initially, there was an initial sense of optimism, which quickly turned to pessimism when Iranian leaders reacted negatively to the proposals (first denying negotiations and then rejecting them). Subsequently, Iranian leaders stated that they did not like the U.S. plans, but offered their own conditions for ending hostilities (5 points).

In this market phase, it seems that Iran wants to maintain its position as a strong geopolitical player, given that it has the ability to flip the switch in the Strait of Hormuz at will. These failed negotiations combined with news of a possible U.S. troop deployment caused investors to continue defending against rising equities.

The Big Picture

The S&P 500 (SP500) continues to decline from all-time highs, with the current correction initially triggered by labor market concerns and now exacerbated by the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz. The technical structure remains fragile and vulnerable to further shocks.

Geopolitical conflict has produced extraordinary price swings in commodities. Prices for crude oil (CL1:COM) fell dramatically based on speculation about the likelihood of a resolution to the conflict. As soon as it became apparent that no agreement had been made regarding a cease fire, the price of crude oil rose significantly. In addition to being uncertain, the price action in commodities demonstrates extreme volatility. Risk-averse investors have turned to traditional safe-haven investments as well as lower-yielding long-dated debt securities that could potentially be affected by higher oil prices and subsequent inflation.

It's also worth noting that precious metals are behaving differently than we would normally expect. They are no longer being used as typical Safe Haven Assets. There has been a tremendous amount of "Speculative Foam" built into the markets. Many investors bought these precious metals at the top of this recent price run-up using leverage (i.e., margin). Now, because there is so much uncertainty in the marketplace, many investors are having to rapidly unwind their leveraged positions. As a result, both gold (XAU) and silver (XAG) are currently the most easily liquidated asset classes for many managers trying to raise capital and immediately increase their portfolio liquidity reserves.

Crude oil prices continue to be positively positioned. Long dated treasury yields have increased, and Bitcoin has fallen further, which supports the view that investor sentiment is extremely risk adverse at present. Investors are experiencing a classic "defensive" shift in equity investment portfolios in response to this unique combination of geopolitical factors. Capital has exited over-valued sectors, including technology and communication services, and moved to other areas of the market where investors can find both income and stability that are typically not negatively impacted by geopolitical shocks or rising commodity prices.

Defensive and cycle-dependent sectors such as materials and utilities are dominating this week, in contrast communication services and technology have experienced heavy losses due to high valuations. It is also worth noting that weakness is systemic and spreading into the broader market. Market breadth is worsening as just 47.2% of the S&P 500 stocks are currently above their 200-day moving average. Generally, when less than half of the market participates in an upward movement, it indicates solid and broad weakness of the market structure.

Therefore, a general repricing of stock values occurring once again due to the geopolitical risk premium will force investors to reduce exposure.

Chart of the Week: Panic Over Oil Supplies (Extreme Backwardation)

This week, I am going to do a deeper dive into a key factor to help us understand why the panic in the energy markets is so severe; it’s known as the term structure of WTI crude oil futures.

From the picture above, we have two major lines. Blue is last month’s price curve and shows a relatively flat price structure. White is this week’s current price action and shows how white starts off very high for very short-dated maturities, peaking at approximately $100 per barrel, but then falls-off rapidly through each of the subsequent year's maturities.

Technical speaking, when short-dated prices (spot) are substantially higher than longer-dated prices (futures), the futures market is said to be in backwardation. On the other hand, if futures prices are slightly higher than spot prices to account for cost of storing and insuring the product, then the futures market is said to be in contango.

I view the extreme and steep backwardation seen in the current oil futures market, similar to past red flags for the world economy. What this extreme backwardation indicates to me is that oil consumers are willing to pay a tremendous premium to obtain barrels today. They fear that there will soon be a disruption in physical supplies caused by U.S.-Iranian tensions and threats against the Strait of Hormuz.

Until such time as the curve returns to its normal shape, the risk of another inflationary shock will cause concern with the Fed and lead to upward pressure on interest rates and limit any sustainable recovery in equity values.

The TurboQuant Enigma

The tech space is always an interesting place to watch markets react; last week was no different with Alphabet/Google (GOOGL) announcing a new AI called TurboQuant which is designed to accomplish all the functions previously done by its predecessors using six time less computer memory than before. And almost immediately after the announcement came a selloff of shares of several key memory component manufactures such as Micron (MU) and Samsung (SSNLF), as investors were fearful that since the new AI uses six time less memory that there will be significantly lower demand for physical chips.

If AI is going to become lighter and a lot cheaper to operate then it will likely be utilized far more than it would otherwise. AI can run on standard PCs and common hardware, and no longer do you have to tie your device to a server or spend an arm and leg. With this explosion of utilization of AI, we are bound to see an upward trend in total demand for those technological parts.

So in summary, the increased use of AI will create a huge surge in demand for these types of technological components, not less demand. Looking back at the Google stock chart I see that the drop in the share price has been extremely rapid and abnormal.

Using some technical terms, we could say that the RSI indicator has fallen into what’s known as oversold territory. To my way of thinking, the panic selling has gone too far and created a possible buying opportunity for those investors willing to take a longer view and ignore their emotions.

The Road Ahead

For the upcoming week, my outlook will be very guarded. The S&P 500 is currently below it’s 200 day moving average; as you know, historical trends suggest that when stocks trade below their 200 day moving averages, there could be a higher level of volatility than normal. Therefore, the primary focus of the week will be on the effects that the ongoing conflict with Iran has on inflation and what direction the Federal Reserve will take. There is also much scheduled activity on the economic calendar. Consumer Confidence data will be reported on Tuesday. Due to increasing gasoline prices and consequently increasing limitations on discretionary household spending, I do not anticipate an increase in consumer confidence.

Additionally, JOLTS (Job Openings And Labor Turnover Survey) job postings data for February is expected to reveal some carry-over strength in the labor market prior to the war. An estimate of 7 million jobs available is anticipated.

The ISM manufacturing index will be released on Wednesday. I am anticipating expansion in the sector to have occurred again in March, however, I will be watching the price component of the data closely. Energy cost increases globally are occurring as a result of the current energy shock.

The most significant event of the week will occur on Friday: the release of the employment report for March. Based on current trends and estimates, I expect non-farm payroll additions of approximately 80,000, resulting in a stable unemployment rate of 4.4 percent. Although markets appear to be pricing-in potential interest-rate hikes by the Federal Reserve during 2026, I believe those estimates may be overly optimistic based on future data indicating declines in demand in subsequent months.

In terms of currency and bonds, I expect the U.S. dollar to continue to gain traction as a safe-haven asset until evidence of a resolution occurs regarding the ongoing war. Concurrently, I anticipate that the flattening of the Treasury yield curve will continue into April if oil prices remain high throughout May, as investors increasingly view near-term inflation expectations as increasing.

data.title: Legence Corp.'s Surge Justifies A Respectful Downgrade
data.description:
March 27th was a surprising day when it comes to Legence Corp. (LGN). Even though management reported financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year, the share price barely budged. This was in spite of the fact that revenue came in well above what analysts anticipated and management increased guidance for the 2026 fiscal year. In all likelihood, these positives were offset against weaker than expected profitability. However, cash flows for the company are growing nicely, and the future looks bright from an operational standpoint. With that in mind, however, I must say that I am no longer all that bullish on the company from an investment standpoint.

To be clear, the company is a high-quality operator that just completed a major acquisition. Other acquisitions have been occurring also, including a rather small one in the amount of $30 million. In the long run, I suspect that the company will do just fine, especially as it leans into the data center space. However, shares are no longer as cheap as they were. After all, the stock has jumped 29% since I called it a ‘buy’ in November of last year. Over that same window of time, the S&P 500 is down 5%. In light of its current valuation, I believe that downgrading it to a ‘hold’ makes sense here.

A Mixed Quarter And A Bright Future

On the morning of March 27th, the management team at Legence announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Things actually went incredibly well, with revenue totaling $737.6 million. That's an increase of 34.5% over the $548.2 million that the company reported the previous year. Revenue actually ended up being $118.3 million above what analysts were hoping to see. As great as the increase in revenue was, however, the bottom line for the firm could have been a bit better. Earnings per share actually improved from negative $0.57 to negative $0.55. But that still ended up falling short of analysts’ expectations to the tune of $0.59.

When you dig deeper, you see that even though the profitability on a per-share basis improved, the overall net loss of the company widened from $18.7 million to $32.7 million. There were multiple reasons behind this. For instance, the company incurred about $4.8 million of acquisition-related expenses and $2.4 million in long-lived asset impairments. There were other disparities as well, such as those involving income taxes, non-controlling interests, and $3.8 million incurred during the quarter associated with indemnification asset adjustments that management attributed to unrecognized tax benefits acquired in a prior acquisition. As disappointing as this bottom line figure was, the rest of the metrics actually improved markedly year over year. Operating cash flow soared from $6.1 million to $94.8 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a jump from $23.7 million to $67.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company increased from $56.8 million to $87 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2025 in their entirety compared to 2024. Once again, revenue improved while the bottom line worsened. Cash flows, however, increased without exception. I do think that a discussion of what is going on here is in order. And perhaps the best place to start with when it comes to this would be its Installation & Maintenance segment. This is the largest part of the company, accounting for 76.6% of sales in the final quarter of the year. This part of the company focuses on fabricating and installing HVAC systems, process piping, and other types of systems in new and existing industrial, commercial, and institutional facilities. It also provides customers with preventative and corrective maintenance services for those systems.

Year over year for the final quarter, this segment benefited from a surge in demand associated with data centers and other technology customers, as well as those in the life sciences and healthcare segments. This helped to push revenue up from $391.3 million to $565.1 million. In general, Legence has benefited a lot from the data centers and technology space. For the company as a whole, revenue in the final quarter associated with these clients amounted to $377.9 million. That was significantly above the $209.3 million reported the previous year. Life sciences and healthcare revenue also grew year over year from $89.7 million to $120.1 million.

By comparison, the Engineering & Consulting segment grew at a much more modest pace, with revenue expanding from $156.9 million to $172.6 million. And unlike the Installation & Maintenance segment, which saw profitability grow from $61.4 million to $99.7 million, this segment reported a small decline in profits from $51.5 million to $47.8 million. But honestly, at this point, it really is the Installation & Maintenance segment that is doing the heavy lifting.

In the future, we can expect this to be the case also. The firm ended 2025 with $3.67 billion of backlog. This was well above the $2.47 billion that it had the previous year. 49% of the company's backlog at the time involved data centers and technology clients. That's up from 31% the previous year. All other categories declined, even the life sciences and healthcare space. But in some respects, this was just because of how disproportionately fast-growing the data centers and technology category was.

It is necessary to point out that none of this includes recent acquisition activities. The biggest of these was The Bowers Group, which operates as a mechanical contractor in Maryland that provides customers with mechanical and plumbing solutions for complex building systems. This particular business generates revenue of about $767 million. And it brought to the table another $1.3 billion of backlog. Management ended up paying $475 million for this acquisition, $325 million of which was in cash, $100 million of which was in stock, and the remaining $50 million of which is deferred consideration in the form of cash or stock that management was able to choose at the time that the deal closed. They revealed to shareholders that they decided to pay this out in stock. And honestly, considering how the stock is priced, I think that was a wise move.

For 2026, it's expected that The Bowers Group will generate between $825 million and $875 million in revenue. From that, EBITDA should amount to between $75 million and $85 million. This acquisition is instrumental in pushing up revenue for the company for this year. That's why management expects sales to climb from $2.55 billion last year to between $3.7 billion and $3.9 billion this year. That represents an upward revision from the $3.5 billion to $3.7 billion previously anticipated. And some of that seems to reflect another acquisition that management announced was completed on March 1st of this year. That happens to be Metrix Engineers, which serves as an MEP engineering company focused on the education market in the Pacific Northwest. That ultimately cost shareholders $30 million.

From its revenue for 2026, the company anticipates EBITDA of between $400 million and $430 million. At the midpoint, this would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of $249.9 million. Keeping in mind the fact that its purchase of The Bowers Group was not completed until subsequent to the end of the final quarter, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are priced using both results from 2025 and estimates for 2026. There are severe comparability issues when it comes to the 2025 figures considering the timing of its acquisition activities. So I do think that relying on the 2026 forward-looking figures is the wise move to make. But even with that in consideration, the stock is not exactly cheap on an absolute basis. And in that chart, you can see that while it is cheaper than most other similar firms, it's not the cheapest of the group. Of course, I do put more weight on the absolute valuation of the company than I do on relative valuation. And the fact of the matter is that, after the stock price rose so much, it no longer looks as appealing as it once did.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available at this time, it is my belief that Legence is fine, but it's not exceptional. It is a very solid company that has a bright future ahead for it. But given the run-up in pricing that we saw, I believe that taking a more cautious approach and assigning it a ‘hold’ rating is the appropriate course of action.

data.title: MLPI Vs. AMLP: Why NEOS Is The New Leader Among Midstream ETFs
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The NEOS MLP & Energy Infrastructure High Income ETF (MLPI) caught my attention immediately when the new product from the NEOS team was launched. Also, after looking at and comparing similar funds investing in midstream assets, which readers often recommended I pay special attention to, MLPI stood out. The lack of a track record, though, kept me from taking a practical interest in this asset. Since we now have the first results after three months, which allow us to assess MLPI’s performance, here’s my attempt to compare it with the sector’s benchmark—the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP).

Midstream assets have proven to be more volatile over this short period, which is primarily due to rising energy prices. Since we are currently in a bull market, we can immediately find an answer to the question regarding whether NEOS funds are subject to a "growth ceiling." Comparing the total returns of the MLPI and the AMLP over a 3-month period shows that the former outperformed the latter by 4.36%. Therefore, in practice, these theoretical disadvantages are offset by the effective actions of NEOS portfolio managers. The comparison of MLPI and AMLP in this article confirms that the former fund deserves the status of "top performer" among MLP and Midstream assets.

Fundamental Similarities Between MLPI and AMLP

Every investor should understand the actual structure of these investment vehicles, as although MLPI and AMLP are two popular ETFs that invest in MLP assets, their internal workings differ significantly. Nevertheless, it is worth noting their common "genetic code" first.

  • Firstly, the two funds are pure-play MLP holdings in the midstream sector. Because of this, the structure is classified as a C-Corporation.
  • Secondly, the funds' assets do not include any companies in the E&P sector. Therefore, there is 67% overlap in their holdings. As a result, the price movements of their shares are highly correlated.
  • Thirdly, investors who hold shares in these funds receive Form 1099-DIV instead of the complex Form K-1. MLPI and AMLP can therefore be held in IRA/401k retirement accounts without UBTI issues.
  • Finally, these funds are primarily suited for investors focused on high dividend income who want to invest in assets with relatively low correlation to the S&P 500.

Fundamental Differences Between MLPI and AMLP

An in-depth analysis of these ETFs reveals the criteria by which they should be distinguished. Firstly, their asset ownership structure differs: the NEOS managers employ an active management strategy that incorporates a covered call strategy. A distinctive feature of this strategy is the sale of out-of-the-money options on selected MLPs, generating additional returns from the option premium. In this regard, the total return on MLPI is higher than that of AMLP. Although when we talk about MLPI, what we mean first and foremost is investing in MLPs, it actually includes a mixed portfolio where the share of partnerships is no more than 25%, and the remaining 75% consists of shares in energy corporations, among which are Canadian issuers.

By the way, it makes MLPI similar to another fund, the Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX), the one I compared to AMLP earlier. It was because of the diversified portfolio structure that I chose MLPX, thinking it would have a higher total return by 2026. From the time of publication on January 5, 2026, the total return for MLPX was 24.94%, whereas AMLP’s was only 15.41%. I think this 9.53% advantage is due precisely to portfolio diversification, a feature that is also typical of MLPI. Because NEOS has a wider selection of managers, it can significantly diversify its portfolio across assets; it has 27 holdings in its portfolio, while the top 10 assets account for 63.34% of the portfolio.

Portfolio managers at AMLP, in contrast, favor passive portfolio management, which tracks an index comprising the largest MLPs. I think this management approach is less effective since it doesn't leave room for maneuver or flexibility when rebalancing the portfolio during the market's most volatile periods, like February-March 2026. As the choice is limited to midstream companies in this sector, this portfolio is less diversified (only 13 holdings) and significantly more concentrated (the top 10 assets account for 93.67%).

There is a more significant difference between the funds in their dividend policies, which favor MLPI. For one thing, NEOS pays monthly dividends, as opposed to quarterly dividends like AMLP. Furthermore, based on information from the MLPI website, dividend yield for the year is 14.92%, twice that of AMLP. Information provided on the Seeking Alpha website includes the yield based on 3 monthly payments.

Forming the dividend policy of the funds, on the other hand, impacts tax efficiency, with MLPI having a clear advantage over AMLP. Since the latter is a C-Corp, corporate tax (21%) is payable. In a rising market, additional tax burdens arise, and the managers of MLPI, for example, reduce these through structuring as a RIC. To maintain this tax status, a fund like NEOS must not exceed a 25% allocation to MLPs in its portfolio. Overall, based on Form 8937 for 2025, both funds have a share of ROC in their distributions; but while AMLP's share is minimal (6.68%), that of MLPI is much higher (77.09%). Because of this, MLPI shareholders can defer their tax liabilities until they sell their shares.

This results in a difference in total returns between the funds, where MLPI has the advantage. Despite the fact that data is only available starting December 17, 2025 (start of MLPI operations), it is already possible to identify a 4.36% advantage for the NEOS fund (20.98% vs. 16.62%).

The table below provides a more detailed comparison of MLPI and AMLP.

Risks of the Investment Thesis

There are risks associated with investing in MLPI and AMLP due to trends in the Midstream sector. We should keep in mind the potential risks arising from the rising yield on 10-year U.S. Treasury bonds. Such a trend could trigger capital outflows from assets like MLPI and AMLP. As of now, yield has increased to 4.456%. Should this growth trend continue and yield rise to 5%, investments in Midstream companies would become unprofitable. Among the factors driving bond yield growth are inflation risks, who are still a thing since oil and natural gas prices are crazy high. According to CME data, there's a chance the Fed might take a tougher stance when reviewing monetary policy and the key rate, something the market is already pricing in.

An additional risk stems from the volume of energy resources transported by Midstream companies, whose revenues are derived up to 90% from capacity booking. Even though oil and natural gas prices do not directly impact these companies’ financial results, an indirect effect exists due to the increased threat of stagflation and economic recession. This would lead to a collapse in demand amid persistently high inflation, further reducing capacity commitments under new contracts. Ultimately, energy transport volumes would decline, resulting in lower revenue for Midstream companies.

Conclusion

I compared the key criteria of MLPI and AMLP in this article, and concluded that although the NEOS fund is a newcomer to this market, they're the best choice among midstream ETFs. The advantages of MLPI include a structured approach to portfolio composition, higher payouts, and a lower tax burden. In addition, its portfolio is better suited for a bull market, allowing it to gain a 4.36% outperformance over AMLP in just 3 months. The NEOS management team has thus managed to overcome the "growth ceiling" barrier, allowing it to generate double-digit dividend yields without sacrificing market growth potential. Thus, my preference is for MLPI, which, regardless of the current macroeconomic risks, enjoys a number of advantages that position it as the new "gold standard" among midstream ETFs.

data.title: LENZ Therapeutics: Q4 Results Highlight A Worrying Lack Of Market Demand (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Lenz Therapeutics (LENZ) is an early-stage biotechnology company based in Solana Beach, CA. Its main product is VIZZ (aceclidine ophthalmic solution) 1.44%, marketed as ‘the first and only aceclidine-based eye drop for the treatment of presbyopia’.

The word presbyopia has ancient Greek roots, literally meaning “old man’s eye”. It is a condition where, as we age, our near-sighted vision worsens. While not everyone is diagnosed with the condition, it is considered to affect almost everyone as they age, with most people developing symptoms in their 40s or 50s. It is not a disease, but instead a natural, progressive age-related process whereby the eye loses its ability to focus on nearby objects due to a loss of flexibility and elasticity in the crystalline lens. It is why reading becomes more challenging as we age, particularly in low light.

The standard treatments for presbyopia are glasses, bifocals, or contact lenses. Collectively, this is an enormous global market; Fortune Business Insights estimates it as more than $180 billion. It is also worth noting that traditional LASIK surgery done on both eyes is not intended to fix presbyopia, as the technique is for fixing far-sighted vision. However, options like monovision/multifocal ablation which use lasers to set one eye for near vision and the other for far vision can fix the symptoms temporarily. I say temporarily because, again, presbyopia is age-related and progressive so, as years pass post-surgery, patients will likely require reading glasses anyway.

Estimates suggest 1.8 billion people globally have presbyopia – or 128 million Americans – but almost everyone beyond a certain age develops the condition even if they never realize it or get an official diagnosis. In any event, what is certain is that the condition is extremely common among adults in their middle/late stage of life.

Vizz received FDA approval in July 2025 as an eye-drop formula that can be used once daily to provide temporarily relief from presbyopia.

The mechanism is quite simple. The aceclidine formula gently squeezes the iris muscle to constrict the pupil and create a pin-hole effect. What this means is that there is less stray light entering the eye which creates a larger depth of focus. While the drops do not improve the flexibility of the lens – the cause of presbyopia – they nevertheless have the effect of improving the clarity of near objects by making your pupils smaller (the opposite of dilation) to about 2mm in size. As shown in the graphic below, the drops react within a few minutes with benefits lasting 8-10 hours for most people.

The market for treatment of presbyopia is estimated at $11 billion in 2025 and expected to reach $18 billion by 2036. Lenz’s product offers the potential to top into this market. In their latest earnings call, management noted they were ‘activating targeted consumer campaigns to establish VIZZ as a compelling alternative to reading glasses’, which is an ambitious objective. Their pricing model offers the potential for a big payday should the market demand materialize as the company expects. Thinking of the US pool of presbyopia sufferers (128 million), if even 1% of the market adopt the product, this would generate $1.024 billion per year using the ~$800 annual payment figure suggested by the company below. A larger (10%) penetration would yield something like $10.24 billion annually.

Management’s own estimates are for a total addressable market of around $3 billion per year, which – using these price points – suggests they can capture around 3% of the total US presbyopia sufferers. Interestingly, in their latest investor presentation, they propose a market penetration rate of 6%, which suggests they are anticipating lower ASPs going forward.

In terms of their addressable market and potential annual revenue figure, given the company’s current market cap of around $285 million, this would suggest the company is thoroughly undervalued at present. In this article, I’d like to assess their market demand in more detail and provide an update on my previous article – my first coverage of Lenz – back in December 2025.

Updating my Coverage

In my initial coverage of Lenz, I issued a buy rating based on the fairly straightforward idea that the market was underestimating the product’s potential market adoption and that the recent share-price performance – shares had fallen nearly 70% in just two months – was an overreaction to some negative news the company experienced just prior to its publication. While I was somewhat skeptical of VIZZ customer demand, I nevertheless believed that the market had sufficiently priced this risk into its shares and that VIZZ could not only steal market share from its competition – mainly Vuity from Abbvie (ABBV) (FDA approved in 2021) and Qlosi from Orasis Pharmaceuticals (approved in 2023; launched in 2025) – but also from those who use the standard treatment options of eye glasses and, especially, once-daily contact lenses. Indeed, the data suggested that VIZZ was a superior product to its competition (esp. Vuity and Qlosi) in terms of efficacy, as shown below. Also included by comparison was Yuvezzi, which was recently approved by the FDA by Tenpoint Therapeutics, and is due to launch very soon.

Vuity also had a less than spectacular take-up in its first full year of availability (2022); they filled just 190,000 prescriptions among 120,000 patients, possibly indicating that only 70,000 (56%) took it again after their first trial. Perhaps rather too optimistically, given its stronger reported efficacy and safety/tolerability, I suspected that Vuity’s inferiority was the reason for its slow take-up—a problem that VIZZ could solve. However, in doing so, I may have overestimated the product demand by believing in the company’s own market research too much, while underestimating potential customer rejections of the eye drops due to cost, safety, comfort, or other concerns.

I admit this buy recommendation was poor, with shares now trading more than 45% lower after its publication, and a full 90% below the market high in October 2025. With shares now below $9, shareholder value has been obliterated in the space of less than 6 months.

Let’s examine their most recent earnings to assess product uptake, before offering a reassessment of the market demand.

Most Recent Earnings

Lenz reported Q4 and full year 2025 earnings on March 24th. The company announced product revenue of $1.6 million through 20,000 prescriptions in Q4 alone, which was the first full quarter of sales after launching in October 2025. Lenz management expect 45,000 prescriptions in the first 6 months, which could translate to 90-100,000 prescriptions in the first twelve months if they keep up this pace. That would generate $8 million or so for the year, but this could be a big “if”. As a reminder, Abbvie’s product Vuity filled 190,000 prescriptions in the first year, so Vizz has some serious catching up to do to reach this figure.
