By comparison, therefore, the figure of 20,000 was disappointing despite what management said in the earnings call about its outperformance compared to Vuity. “The market” seemed to agree with me here, with shares falling 18% the following day. The low revenue figure of $1.6 million was compounded by $39.6 million in S,G&A expenses in the quarter. According to management, 80% of this was for sales and marketing, comprised mainly of increases in headcount, primarily an 88-territory sales force, alongside several advertising campaigns. This included a direct-to-consumer (DTC) campaign involving celebrity actress Sarah Jessica Parker for this ad among several TV appearances.

These higher costs led to a Q4 net loss of $35.9 million for the quarter, or $1.16 per share, and a quarterly cash burn of $33 million. This brought the full year 2025 total net loss to $70 million.

The company paid its management and staff $3.68 million in stock-based compensation. When SBC is more than double total product sales, that raises a red flag. It is certainly something to watch for going forward. They also issued $80 million in common stock in the 4th quarter alone (though Seeking Alpha shows $149 million issued in the TTM period).

This has added meaningfully to the significant shareholder dilution already experienced to date.

The company has already burned through $354 million over the course of its history – as far as I can calculate – so they have a long way to go before getting back above water, and until they can meaningfully bring in $100 million or so in a quarter to offset their higher S,G&A expenses, SBC (which continues to rise), and secondary share offerings that dilute shareholders, I see further punishment ahead for shareholders. And let’s be frank—they are a long way from that revenue figure.

In the earnings report, management also noted it was continuing to expand its sales force from 88 to 117 territories, after having already secured ‘broad uptake by eye care professionals (“ECPs”), with over 6,500 unique prescribers in Q4 2025, on pace to grow to over 10,000 through Q1 2026’. They also noted that ‘over 55% of prescribing ECPs have written multiple times’, which is in line with what Abbvie saw in its first year, as noted above. Again, I did not think this result was particularly impressive, though it is important to give this company time to demonstrate the market demand throughout its first full year. Only then can we know what kind of customer retention rate we might see going forward. If it stays around 55%, that might suggest significantly weaker demand than expected. It means, in effect, that only half of those who try the eye drops continue to use them beyond the first initial prescription.

In this regard, let me outline my own concerns as I have spent more time now investigating this market. If they are seriously considering their VIZZ product as a disruptor to reading glasses or contact lenses, it makes sense for us to compare the pros and cons here.

Costs

In terms of cost, basic non-prescription reading glasses cost $10-30 from Walmart (WMT) or Amazon (AMZN), with even the more expensive ones from places like Warby Parker (WRBY) between $100-300. These are stylish, fashionable, comfortable and driven by high technology in the lenses, offering bifocals or Progressive Addition Lenses ("PALs") that offer a gradual transition in the lens, thereby treating distance vision, near vision, and everything in between. Even for these high-end glasses, the total for the lenses and frames would be $400-600. So long as the person wearing them looks after them, they can last several years with the same lenses, which places them as a much more cost-effective option than daily eye drops that carry recurring monthly or quarterly charges.

Of course, there are those who can’t or don’t want to wear glasses for a number of reasons including comfort or practicality. This is the small market that VIZZ is trying to tap into, but it is up against the incumbent: contact lenses. Even the high-end contact lenses with the most expensive prescriptions cost around $400-600 per year for the monthlies or up to $1000 per year for the dailies. There might be additional expenses when adding in solutions, exams and fitting, but only the solutions would be recurring. In any event, contact lenses are now in many cases covered by standard health insurance packages, and have been for years. As far as I can tell, VIZZ is not. It even highlights this on their website FAQ page (seen below). Patients wanting coverage must use their FSA or HSA plans, but these may not provide full coverage.

This means most people will have to pay either all or a portion of the ~$800 per year out-of-pocket, and I’m not sure there will be a large number of people willing to do this on a permanent basis, when there are cheaper options available. Some of course will, but we’re likely talking about a small minority of those for whom money or healthcare spending is less of a problem. CCO Shawn Olsson admitted as much in the latest earnings call when outlining their target market:

‘So we know what these patients look like, and we knew that prior to launch, right? We knew that they make over $100,000 a year. They’re mostly between 45 and 65. They’re in major city centers.’

As such, the sense that most presbyopes would be priced out is clearly anticipated, which makes much of their data on market demand – outlined below – subject to critique.

Health Risks (Serious)

I spent some time in my first write-up investigating the price drop that occurred last year as a result of news being released about an adverse event for VIZZ, whereby one patient had a full retina tear. This is a very serious problem that could potentially lead to blindness. The company acknowledged this incident as an opportunity to learn and reevaluate its prescribing methods, noting that physicians in future need to do a more comprehensive background health check to assess for high-risk patients before prescribing the medication. Nevertheless, the incident exposes the health risks that come with eye drops in general, and with those containing powerful ingredients that could cause irritation or react in some ways with a person’s physiology.

On their customer advisory information page, they offer the following warnings and precautions for VIZZ, including a retinal tear as a possibility.

According to the FDA’s Adverse Event Monitoring System database – which can be found here (search “VIZZ” by product name, then click “Listing of Cases” and “Serious”) – in total, there have been 12 reported serious adverse events among patients using the product. We can see the list below, including the full retinal tear.

Most recently, several patients have experienced vitreous detachment, which is not uncommon, often not harmful – it causes floaters in the eye or sometimes temporary flashes (photopsia) – but could lead in odd cases to a retinal tear. It is certainly serious enough to be classed, officially, as a “serious” adverse event.

These adverse events have occurred in patients prescribed the drug post-approval, and not by those who were involved in clinical trials in controlled settings. This makes the slide (below) which appears in their latest investor presentation particularly troubling to me.

While it is entirely accurate to report that ‘There were no reported retinal tears or detachments with VIZZ in clinical trials’ – because this was true – I feel it would have been more appropriate for the company to have provided more up-to-date information that does not omit facts about the serious adverse events that patients, post-approval, have experienced (including 1 retinal tear and 6 detachments). Note: the last of these serious incidents was reported on Feb 16th 2026, which may have been after the publication of their latest investor presentation. The other 5 would certainly have been known at the time though.

I was also left feeling uncomfortable with the statement ‘<4% discontinuation due to treatment-related adverse events’. Again, I’m sure this is factually correct, but given that 20% of users experienced instillation site irritation, 16% dim vision, 8% conjunctival hyperemia (specific “red eye”), 7% ocular hyperemia (general “red eye”), and 13% experienced headaches (19% of these more than "mild"), I find it very hard to believe that more than only a fraction of these people would continue using the product on a regular basis. So why the low number then?

These figures are from the trial results, and not from the general population of users post-approval. As someone who has undergone a drug trial before, I can tell you that there is a strong sense of responsibility that comes with it. Patients are perhaps more likely to tolerate any manageable health concerns as they are considered temporary; they may feel a sense of guilt if they discontinue use before the trial concludes. Among the general population, when not being trialed, however, I would suggest the discontinuation rate would be much higher. They would feel no obligation to continue using the medication if it wasn’t easily tolerated, especially given the other options seem very reasonable and even more tolerable in the case of glasses. Indeed, the 55% repeat prescription figure cited above suggests that I might be correct here. Call it a hunch. Indeed, Lenz admitted that only 75% of their 223 phase 1/2 trial participants would continue to use VIZZ after the study. But let’s remember that this was Lenz researchers collecting the data rather than someone independent.

In any case, the company did not disclose in their investor presentation that a dozen users have reported experiencing a serious adverse reaction. Again, FDA approval came in July last year, so the company had 7 months of post-approval data from thousands of users by the time they put together their slide deck.

Health Risks (Mild)

Turning to dim vision, which carried a 16% precedence rate (with 8% of these more than “mild”), this is also a concern given that night-driving could be impacted by using VIZZ. This is likely to be a more serious problem during winter months, and especially in the northern US, when days are shorter and nightfall comes earlier. If someone uses the eye drops at 9am for example, the effects could last 8-10 hours, meaning that by the time they drive home for work, it is dark out. The pinhole effect created by the eyedrops mean that less light enters the eye. While this helps the eye to focus on near objects, it can be a problem when there is less natural or ambient light around. I would suspect, based on simple logic, that anyone experiencing dim vision and difficulty with night-driving as a consequence of using VIZZ would either stop using the product or cut down its use during the winter months, which would of course hurt both sales and the product’s reputation/usefulness.

In all, it must be said that glasses bring next to no health risks such as these. Contact lenses have been known to cause eye infections, conjunctivitis (pink eye), corneal ulcers and vision loss in extreme cases, but proper care, maintenance, and hygiene relieve almost all these risks. This presents VIZZ as a higher risk solution to presbyopia.

Taking the differences in cost and safety together, I posit that it is likely to be only a very small proportion of the population that will feel VIZZ eyedrops are the best solution for them. This is my main concern for their market; simply put, it is not as large as Lenz management think it is. So, while – in the best-case scenario – they may come to corner their market, stripping away any dominance that Vuity or Qlosi has at this point, if too few people want their product due to high cost and health risks, it won’t matter if theirs is the best. As a friend of mine in the investing community reminds me: if you own the only movie theatre in town but no one wants to see a movie, your dominant position is irrelevant. Even if Lenz come to control the supply, their has to be a demand for them to profit from it.

While I was willing to give them the benefit of the doubt in my last review – they had not delivered any product sales at that point – the most recent earnings highlighted what I feared most: little market demand for their product.

Of course, this is compounded because as of 2026 Lenz is a one-trick pony. For Q4 2025, their R&D expenses fell to $0 as they have gone “all in” on their one product with nothing in the pipeline. This presents an enormous risk. Without sufficient sales of VIZZ, they have nothing to fall back on.

Management noted progress with international expansion, forming a relationship with Lotus Pharmaceutical helping to provide an 8-country license in Southeast Asia and with Lunatus to focus on the Middle East. They submitted applications to the European health authority and have plans for UK and Canada going forward. CFO Daniel Chevallard announced in the call:

‘In total, 5 ex U.S. NDA or equivalent submissions have been completed, and we anticipate over 10 to be completed by the end of this year with multiple potential approvals possible in 2027.’

This is positive news but so much of their long-term potential is riding on market demand in the US, its most important market to penetrate at this stage. On this point, I raise some concerns.

Reassessing Market Demand

According to a Lenz commissioned survey of 1,358 presbyopes, over 60% ‘would consider an eye drop solution’. From this statistic and their estimation of the potential size of the market – 128 million presbyopes in the US – Lenz management have speculated a $3 billion market opportunity and ‘blockbuster potential’ with VIZZ.

Lenz suggested the proposed 6% adoption rate was ‘conservative’, yet this represents 8 million people in the US, which is actually an enormous number of people. As investors, we should ask the blunt question: would 8 million people prefer to ditch their glasses or contact lenses, which are significantly cheaper and carry fewer health considerations, for a once-daily eye drop that provides temporary relief for an age-related, progressive condition that has already been “fixed” by other methods for decades? If so, should we short EssilorLuxottica (ESLOY) and Warby Parker shares? It would make sense to if we believed these projections, but, personally, I do not.

My confidence (of lack of) stems from a reassessment of market demand based on Vuity revenue. If a decent-sized market existed for VIZZ, we would see glimpses of this in Vuity revenues, as one of only two other eye-drop medications on the market (the other being Qlosi, launched in April 2025). In their latest earnings release, Abbvie did not mention Vuity at all in their 12-page prepared remarks and financial statements; nor was it discussed on the earnings call itself or in the Q&A session. I went back and scanned several earnings call transcripts and annual reports, and nothing about Vuity was said, going back several quarters. Then I came across an article that noted:

‘In September 2022, AbbVie decided to reduce sales and marketing activities around Vuity, contributing to a pre-tax impairment charge of $770 million in the third quarter of 2022.’

This showed up in Abbvie’s Q4 and full year 2022 earnings and was discussed in their 10-K annual report, noting:

‘In September 2022, the company made a strategic decision to reduce ongoing sales and marketing investment related to Vuity, an on-market product to treat presbyopia.’

One can only assume the product was not selling well, which then begs the following questions in relation to VIZZ:

  • Can we assume that the poor performance of Vuity was related to the product itself and not weak market demand for the product line in general?
  • How much confidence should we have that VIZZ will outperform Vuity and that the former will buck the trend in this regard, providing the market solution that Vuity was not able to?

Indeed, the differences between the two products were not vast. Certainly, Vuity was less efficacious (did not last as long; required two drops per day instead of one) and had a superior safety profile, but this does not necessarily translate to strong market demand. I keep coming back to this point because it is crucial.

Lenz noted in their latest earnings call that they anticipate their DTC campaign will take several quarters to roll out and ‘translate into prescription trends’. CEO Evert Schimmelpennink added:

‘What we are seeing today are encouraging early indicators of that process, and we would expect a more meaningful impact on script volume as we move into the second half of the year.’

The company noted that 5 months into launch, the unaided awareness of their product stands at 79%, which seems rather high to me. But the figure might be working against them, because if so many people know about the product but the prescriptions numbers are so low, then that might be the clearest indication yet that the market demand is just not there.

Investors must keep a close eye on these figures, but I have strong reservations here. I do not believe the company has a moat around its business, or any kind of competitive advantage, therefore little pricing power. Moreover, I remain unconvinced by the market size. I have no reason to believe that Lenz will get anywhere near the 6% adoption rate (8 million users), which would afford the company a revaluation and send shares upward.

Financial and Valuation Considerations

Lenz ended the quarter with $292 million in cash and equivalents and next to no debt (just $350,000), which gives them a decent runway of something like 2-3 years if the current quarterly cash burn is sustained. So, while the company has more cash than their entire market cap – cash per share of $9.40 with a current share price of $8.94 (as of March 26th closing price) – the market is clearly pricing in this weak demand already. This is a classic value trap and will continue to be viewed as such until they can mitigate the one, major concern here: weaker than expected market demand for VIZZ.

Lenz carries a PB ratio around 1 and a current PS ratio of 14.7 and an EV/sales ratio around 11. It is hardly cheap from a revenue perspective, but certainly looks cheap from a balance sheet (book value) perspective. However, this is misleading given the company will almost certainly continue to burn through cash over the next year and possibly into 2027, thereby impacting shareholder equity. In fact, the path to reaching FCF positive might take several years by which time the company may have to dilute shareholders still further with additional debt financing.

Short interest is now around 28% according to Seeking Alpha, which is very high and should raise some red flags for investors, prospective and current. Some insider buying in November last year (CFO Daniel Chevallard and director James Mccollum) helped propel the stock higher but almost immediate market selling from another insider (CCO Shawn Olsson) sends a questionable message to the investment community. Shares were trading above $20 at that point. Now they are valued at less than half that, but they have not been bought up by management.

Taking their most recent earnings results into consideration, I have now turned bearish on the company and its prospects going forward. I had intended to give them the benefit of the doubt, but I see further downside ahead given their less than convincing market penetration to date. Investors and analysts alike will have several opportunities to reassess market demand through product sales and prescription figures, but as it stands, I see them underperforming their own expectations and the general market. From initiating Lenz with a buy, I am now rating them a sell.

data.title: Amazon's Bears Watch FCF Go Negative But Miss CEO's Lion's Share View
data.description:
My Call On Amazon Is Underwater

Amazon (AMZN) has underperformed the market for some time. It is not unusual for this stock to behave in this way, as its upward trajectory is more choppy compared to other steady stocks whose compounding is more predictable. As Q1 2026 winds down, I want to come back to this stock because I think it suffers from short-sighted bearish narratives from time to time. In particular, at the end of a month where investors' attention suddenly shifted from AI-capex fears to the Strait of Hormuz and the Iran war, this article was triggered by the news that cloud infrastructure emerges as the quiet winner of the AI boom as it offered me an opportunity to stress-test my bullish rating that I issued when the stock was at $240 (which means that this call is currently underwater by roughly 13%).

It might be interesting for some who have not followed me since the early innings that my research on Amazon was triggered by two aspects. First of all, I was struck by the big investment Prime Video was making in The Rings of Power Series. As a result, I analyzed the economics of the series, and I had the opportunity to understand the strength and the stickiness of Amazon's ecosystem. Secondly, being a loyal Costco (COST) shareholder, I realized more and more that Amazon was building something similar, but on a larger scale. As a result, I researched, and I did find that Costco truly influenced Amazon Prime. But then I also understood that Amazon's growth is not only about retail sales or Prime subscriptions. It is about its leading role in AWS, it is about possibly the best online ad business on the planet, and it is also about robotics. In one word. This is why I have argued a few times that we need to evolve Amazon's narrative. Moreover, as the AI race unfolds and the hyperscalers rush to build data centers and create the infrastructure needed to support this compute revolution, I have also become quite interested in Amazon's lethal kiss relationship with NVIDIA, as the company is betting big on its in-house-made chips.

One Misleading Figure

Those who are familiar with Amazon will probably skip this part. But I am surprised by how many people are sometimes deceived by Amazon's headline FCF. Take a look at this table, with the financial numbers in $B.

Year Operating Cash Flow CapEx FCF
2023 84.9 52.8 32.1
2024 115.9 83.0 32.9
2025 139.5 132 7.5
2026E 155.0 (est.) 200.0 (guid.) -45.0

We see that Amazon's FCF can swing by almost $80B in just a few years. At the same time, its operating cash flow doesn't do that, but grows steadily. Therefore, the difference depends solely on Amazon's CapEx.

As we see below, when we put together Amazon's operating cash and CapEx, we see that Amazon's operating cash generally more than covers its CapEx.

This year, however, Amazon will likely report negative FCF, which is the first time since 2022, when the company heavily funded its bet in logistics to separate itself from the rest and create a strong infrastructure moat that has been leveraged since then. Look at the operating cash curve from 2023 onward, and we see that the acceleration is brutal.

Investors know that Amazon doesn't truly care about quarterly figures, but invests heavily in bets that can create long-lasting cash flows. The question is whether the AI CapEx will deliver acceptable returns or not.

To address this issue, we have to understand that Amazon truly has five engines that are often not considered.

The 5 Engines For Amazon's Future

AWS

This is easy. We are talking about the largest cloud business on the planet, with FY25 revenue close to $130B, and a growth rate that is once again speeding up above 20% annually (24% as of Q4 2025). I think what CEO Andy Jassy said during the last earnings call went unnoticed, as investors were busy figuring out Amazon's negative FCF and the possible ROIs on its capex. As I have often said, we need to keep in mind that we are talking about infrastructure, and with this perspective, we can position our portfolios correctly to capture the real megatrend. Jassy said that, currently, AI demand is "barbelled", meaning that it is concentrated at two different ends. On one side, a few AI labs and their viral applications (i.e. OpenAI and ChatGPT, Anthropic and Claude); on the other; those companies that are already getting a lot of value out of AI thanks to the productivity dividend we discussed some time ago, mainly because AI can reduce costs (remember when we talked about the benefits for the banks?). Now, Jassy said that the middle part of the barbell is "the largest and the most durable". This is where all other companies sit, and the new businesses that are being invented thanks to AI. This is why the Amazon CEO believes that:

The lion's share of that demand is yet to come in the middle of that barbell. And that will come over time. It will come as you have more and more companies with AI talent, as more and more people get educated with an AI background, as inference continues to get less expensive, and that's a big piece of what we're trying to do with Trainium and our hardware strategy.

I think that every time Jassy speaks, we get some insights into what is unfolding that we rarely find elsewhere. We also learn that Amazon not only owns immense and valuable data, but can also interpret it rather sharply and communicate the essence of what is going on. The metaphor of the barbell is there to prove and I have not seen many analysts or investors concentrate on the middle of the barbell as the place where demand will explode. This means that we could see AWS double or even triple much sooner than we could imagine (my view is that the inflection point will come between 2028 and 2030, pushing AWS revenues above $400B by 2030). With margins above 30%, we would have $120B in operating cash in 2030.

Advertising

Possibly, this is the most overlooked business among the largest ones that Amazon already runs. It is truly a hidden profit-generating machine. In fact, if you think about it, when you are on amazon.com, you already have in mind to buy something. An ad on Amazon is more valuable than an ad on Google or Meta because the conversion rate is higher. Moreover, Amazon has just started to leverage its proprietary database on its members' behaviors to personalize ads on Prime Video. Here, Amazon has a structural advantage due to closed-loop attribution, which Meta and Google don't have to the same degree.

This is already a $68B business, with extremely high operating margins that, even though they are not disclosed, could be around 70%, considering what other peers are reporting. Considering that it is growing at above 20%, we can expect it to be a $170B business in five years, which means that it will generate almost $120B in operating cash. Do you see where I am headed? In a few years, AWS and advertising could generate over $240B in operating cash, which would be more than enough to cover almost any CapEx cycle.

Custom Chips

Let's now get to what many are still not factoring in. Amazon is developing a chip business (ever heard of Graviton and Trainium?). I challenge you to say right away whether Amazon is losing or making money with chips. You might be surprised that, as of Q4 2025, Trainium and Graviton reported a combined revenue run rate of over $10B, with a triple-digit growth rate YoY. These chips are not tools for internal use; they are a product that Amazon can now sell. In a world hungry for chips, you immediately see how Amazon has done everything it could to be ready and not appear unprepared. Moreover, both Trainium and Graviton (Graviton5, in particular) not only generate revenues for AWS, but they also reduce, at the same time, the cost curve. So, the more chips Amazon sells, the wider its margins become: it earns more revenue while its cost structure becomes leaner. No wonder Amazon is already fully booked.

There is also another big advantage. Amazon Bedrock is the inference platform that the company has developed. Its advantage is that it is LLM-agnostic. This gives AWS an advantage because it offers its customers an infrastructure that can run with whatever models the customers want to use. This is why I say that AWS is a utility, and as its infrastructure develops, workloads are taken away from NVIDIA's supply chain.

Robotics

Amazon has deployed over 1M robots in its fulfillment centers, and we know that robots are involved in or assist around 75% of total deliveries worldwide. Guess how Amazon runs its fleet? Yes, through an AI model: DeepFleet. Once again, efficiency has improved, and costs are also down since the introduction of this model.

Here we have the basis for a true financial case that not many consider. We are all paying attention to the timeline of Tesla's Optimus first deliveries (I addressed this, and, as much as I like Tesla's innovations, I have a hard time believing that the company will deliver coherently to what it has promised so far).

We know that Amazon doesn't like to disclose much about its internal KPIs. But a few months ago, a document leak reported by the New York Times showed that between 2025 and 2027, robots were going to deliver almost $13B in savings or $0.30 per shipped item. In Q3 2024, Jassy talked about 25% reduction in fulfillment time in the Shreveport, Louisiana facility and a 25% cost improvement. What was then tested there as a template is now being rolled out to many other fulfillment centers. So far, robots are not yet impacting Amazon's margins, even though we have seen Amazon's retail margins move up a bit. But now that we are reaching a critical mass, we should see true margin benefits in two main ways: retail cost savings and more AWS revenue (robots require AWS to be managed; the more robots, the more workloads for AWS).

Keep in mind that we have some figures: $13B is not a small amount of money. We can expect robotics to have an impact of around $35B through 2030, not counting what Amazon could do if it decides to sell robots to retail or enterprise customers or offer its DeepFleet model to others.

Zoox

Finally, let's get to robotaxis. Everyone looks at Tesla. Waymo has finally gotten some attention. But Zoox is often forgotten. At the same time, it has already delivered 350k free rides as of March 2026. Waymo is at 400k weekly paid rides across six cities in the U.S., but Zoox seems the credible runner-up. Here, Zoox's CEO said that we should not expect this business to generate profits for a few more years. But we should not ignore what this business is about for Amazon. Yes, it is about moving people without a driver, but it is also a key piece of its logistics empire. Think about the cost savings that autonomous driving can generate in metro areas where Amazon delivers thousands of boxes every day. Zoox is about last-mile synergies. On top of that, it will also develop a robotaxi business. When we view it in this way, we can understand why there is extra margin upside that no DCF can currently capture.

Valuation

Everything I said above flows into one funnel, which we then have to evaluate. We can build a DCF, but I think in Amazon's case, we always have to combine it with an SOTP valuation. Moreover, with Amazon, I prefer to consider a 7-year timeframe, rather than 5, due to how its FCF fluctuates.

My estimate is that Amazon's top-line will be above $1T by 2029 and that it could be around $1.33T in 2032. I also assume that its EBITDA margin will gradually move to 28% and that its CapEx will be at around 10% of its revenues. As a result, I expect Amazon to reach at least $100B in FCF by 2029 and to be around $180B by 2032. I am using a discount rate of 9% (my calculation for its WACC) and a terminal growth rate of 4%.

When we see this, we understand that Amazon is already a bit undervalued considering the base case. But let me show you a sensitivity map that shows how different discount rates and terminal growth rates influence the final share price. We end up with a couple of extremely interesting combinations. For example, if we assume a terminal growth of 4.5%, which, considering that it is a nominal rate with inflation embedded, means that Amazon will only grow at 1.5-2 percentage points above inflation, we see that Amazon could be valued between $278 and $422, depending on the WACC we use (in any case, 8% is not truly low).

Let's use the sum-of-the-parts method for a quick check. I estimate for AWS $49B in EBIT, which I value at a 22x multiple to get an EV of $1.08B. The advertising business does almost $70B in revenue. At a 12x EV/revenue multiple, we have $840B in EV. The retail business could be valued at 1x EV/revenue, which means that we have a value of roughly $520B. For the chips business, I am using an 8x EV/revenue multiple, which means it is worth $80B. Zoox currently doesn't make any money. But I think we could easily say that it is worth around $50B, considering what Tesla is worth, how Waymo is doing, and the strategic value that Zoox would have on Amazon's internal operations. The total enterprise value would be $2.57T, subtracting the net debt of $55B, we have an equity value of $2.52B. Divide this by the 10.7B shares, and we have a share price of $235, which suggests that Amazon is still undervalued. So, the catalyst here clearly becomes the next FCF pivot, when Amazon will prove how its FCF generation power has significantly increased.

Two Strategies While We Wait

After taking some time to study options, I have recently started using them. In Amazon's case, there are two ETFs that could be considered in the current situation. I don't expect Amazon to surge rapidly this year. It is more likely that the stock will either trade within the $180-$220 range, or that it will just go down one quarterly report after the other as investors see the company report negative FCF. In this environment, we can hold Amazon for the long term, but we could expose ourselves to either the Kurv Yield Premium Strategy Amazon ETF (AMZP) or the YieldMax AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF AMZY.

AMZP holds AMZN shares and writes calls at a higher strike. So it does offer us some upside, which is obviously capped at the strike price. But its NAV is linked to AMZN equity and often offers less share price erosion. In the meantime, I think we are in an environment where covered calls can play a meaningful role. AMZN is a great business, but the stock is currently stuck and has had a hard time moving up. Until its CapEx normalizes, AMZP lets us collect yield and even profit if the stock moves up a bit. It is a way to monetize the period we spend holding our Amazon shares without giving them up. And when I say monetizing, I mean that it yields above 20%. The risk is capped upside, while being fully exposed to AMZN's drops.

AMZY, on the other hand, doesn't hold AMZN shares because it uses a synthetic covered call with Treasuries as collateral. It builds its exposure via FLEX options (flexible exchange options, which allow the two counterparties to negotiate terms such as expiry date, strike price, and terms of settlement). It pays a weekly dividend, which currently yields above 50%. But this has a cost: if AMZN moves down, so does AMZY; when AMZN moves up quickly. The goal here is to have weekly income. Currently, the yield is high because volatility on AMZN is high. Volatility means higher option premium that is collected and distributed. If we expect AMZN to trade in a range for some time, as I am, this is a very interesting way to monetize our patience. The risk is capital loss if AMZN drops and NAV erosion when AMZN moves up sharply in a short time. Moreover, this is not a way to own AMZN, but a way to simply cash on Amazon's current volatility, which makes sense if the stock moves sideways.

Conclusion

I am long Amazon and the more I look at it, the more I am convinced the stock is already in value territory, with very mild expectations baked into today's price. Patience has a cost, but there are ways to monetize it through options or by simply increasing the stake in Amazon as its share price is currently below its fair value. Five years from now, I think the chances of being in the red with my positions are extremely low, and this is why I consider Amazon one of the best companies I have a stake in.
